title: podman-remote: pod create: sets wrong CreateCommand
body:
`podman-remote create pod` creates a pod with the wrong `CreateCommand`: it uses the server's argv[], not the client's.
```console
$ ./bin/podman-remote --url unix:/tmp/mypodmansock pod create --name test
626ecf1709e7b977fd11cf64991dce28ffd3edd3e161465a4a5cb956e6c4b6c5
$ ./bin/podman pod inspect --format '{{.CreateCommand}}' test
[/path/to/bin/podman system service --timeout=0 unix:/tmp/mypodmansock]
```
Expected:
```
[/path/to/bin/podman pod create --name test]
```
master @ 1acd2adccb357c317add19cea8f0daea328e8315

comments.body: Is this also a problem with `podman run`? I think it may be...
I mean, with `podman create`. I did not test `podman run`.
I don't consider this a bug. CreateCommand is meant to refer to the process that created the container. In a remote case, the client did not create it but the server.

But isn't it considered the command line used to create the container? Aren't there use cases where you use this information in the systemd stuff, that this could be important?  Can I do podman-remote systemd generate?
Remote systemd is not supported. I consider anything other than running podman locally not supported (including python scripts or go bindings). We really need a podman binary to be executed.

If a field is published, it should be accurate and meaningful.

I'm perfectly fine with `.CreateCommand` being undefined (nil) or 'undefined' (string) or 'N/A' in the remote case, but it should really not be `podman system service etc`

I guess that one way to achieve that would be to get the `os.Args` in `cmd/podman` and just set that in the `SpecGen`. So we don't do that in `libpod`.

This way, we would support `podman-remote` but would avoid storing garbage coming from non-podman clients.

That approach SGTM

On Tue, Jun 23, 2020, 07:44 Valentin Rothberg <notifications@github.com>
wrote:

I think this is resolved by #6896
